name: Ruby Meadows
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site at 6124 ft elevation in central Idaho. Find garnets and quartz on gentle slopes with no permit required.
description:
Ruby Meadows is a scenic BLM public rockhounding site in Idaho County, offering collectors access to garnet and quartz specimens on gentle alpine slopes. The site sits at 6124 feet elevation in the transition zone between the Salmon River country and the Wallowa Mountains geology, where Tertiary granitic intrusions have created excellent mineral-collecting conditions.

This location benefits from the Basin and Range geological influences combined with Idaho's complex mineralization history. The gentle terrain and accessible elevation make it popular with families and intermediate collectors seeking high-quality garnet specimens and quartz crystals. The open meadow environment allows for systematic searching across wide areas without dense forest obstruction.

Expect to find small to medium garnet crystals embedded in weathered granite and quartz-rich soil along the gentle slopes. The best collecting occurs in spring runoff areas and erosion zones where weathering exposes fresh material. The site rewards patience and careful surface scanning rather than aggressive digging.

Collectors appreciate Ruby Meadows for its scenic alpine setting, reasonable accessibility from nearby towns, and the absence of commercial development. The BLM management means free access and simple regulations make this an ideal introduction to Idaho rockhounding.
history: Ruby Meadows sits within Idaho's granitic batholith region, where Cretaceous to Tertiary intrusive activity created the host rocks containing garnet and quartz minerals. The gentle meadow terrain is the result of glaciation and subsequent erosion that has exposed and weathered these mineral-rich granite deposits over millennia, continuously releasing collectible specimens to the surface.
tips: Search the erosion gullies and small washes running through the meadow where weathering concentrates garnet crystals on the surface. Bring a small hand lens to identify garnet's distinctive cubic crystal habit and glassy luster among quartz and feldspar fragments. Focus on areas with reddish soil staining, which often indicates garnet concentration. Early morning light makes spotting the deep red garnets easier against pale granite weathering.
terrain_notes: Gentle sloping alpine meadow at 6124 feet elevation with sparse grass and low brush. Terrain is walkable with good footing across most of the site. Parking to productive collecting areas is typically a short 5-10 minute walk across open ground. Soil is loose and well-drained. No significant rock scrambling required.
written_directions: From Grangeville, take ID 14 east toward the Salmon River country for approximately 18 miles. Turn north on Forest Road 222 (Cabin Creek Road) and follow it for about 8 miles, watching for BLM markers. Ruby Meadows parking area is accessible via a short spur road on the east side of FR 222. High clearance passenger vehicle recommended after spring snowmelt.
road_conditions: Gravel forest road, rough in spring. Passenger vehicle with reasonable clearance acceptable in dry conditions. High clearance or ATV preferred after wet weather.
parking_notes: Small pullout area with space for 3-4 vehicles. No facilities. BLM markers visible from the road.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or mechanized tools. Surface collecting only. Pack out all trash. Stay on BLM land and respect any posted closures.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No mechanized digging tools
Surface collecting only
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Grangeville, ID (approximately 20 miles south) has gas, food, and lodging. Smaller supplies available in Salmon (25 miles east).
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping meadow terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved forest road to parking area requires high clearance vehicle, especially in wet conditions. Short walk from parking to collecting areas. Sturdy shoes recommended for loose volcanic soil.

faq.question: What types of garnets can you find at Ruby Meadows in Idaho?
faq.answer: Ruby Meadows yields primarily almandine garnets, which appear as deep red to reddish-brown crystals ranging from pea-sized to marble-sized specimens. The garnets are found loose in weathered granite soil and are collectible via surface picking. No digging is permitted, so focus on areas with visible red mineral staining.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Ruby Meadows Idaho?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting at Ruby Meadows. It is BLM public land where personal use rockhounding is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You must follow the 25-pound daily limit and collect for personal use only.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to find garnets at Ruby Meadows?
faq.answer: June through September is the optimal season for collecting at Ruby Meadows. Snow typically closes access before June, and heavy rains in spring can make the forest road impassable. Summer weather provides the best access and dry collecting conditions.

faq.question: How far is Ruby Meadows from Grangeville Idaho and what supplies do you need?
faq.answer: Ruby Meadows is approximately 20 miles northeast of Grangeville. Bring a hand lens, collection bag, sturdy hiking boots, and sun protection. No facilities are available at the site, so purchase supplies and fill your vehicle's fuel tank in Grangeville before heading out.

faq.question: Is Ruby Meadows good for beginning rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Ruby Meadows is beginner-friendly and excellent for families with children ages 6 and older. The gentle meadow terrain requires no climbing, and surface collecting is straightforward. Kids enjoy spotting the red garnets in the weathered granite soil without complicated tools or techniques.

faq.question: What tools should you bring for garnet collecting at Ruby Meadows Idaho?
faq.answer: For surface collecting at Ruby Meadows, bring a hand lens for identification, a collection bag or backpack, and comfortable hiking boots. Small flathead screwdrivers can gently free garnets from loose soil, but heavy digging tools and motorized equipment are not permitted. A camera is useful for documenting your finds.

meta_description: Find garnets and quartz at Ruby Meadows, Idaho BLM land. No permit required. Easy alpine collecting near Grangeville. June-September.
